messages.content: You must return JSON only. ALL fields in the schema must be present, even if null or empty.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":72,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Washington DC","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":2,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["travel","football (calcio)"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":32,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":5,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":8,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":38,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":85,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"In my experience, dealing with fake content on social media can be quite challenging, especially for someone like me who spends several hours daily on these platforms. As a retired man living in Washington DC, I've seen firsthand how misinformation can spread like wildfire, causing unnecessary panic and division. My low openness and conscientiousness scores might make me more susceptible to believing false information, but I've learned to be more critical over time. I remember when I first saw a story about a famous footballer (calcio) being involved in a scandal. It turned out to be completely false, but it had already caused a lot of harm. That's when I realized the importance of verifying information before sharing it. I've since made it a habit to check the source of the information and look for other reliable sources that confirm the story. I also try to avoid sharing anything that seems too sensational or too good to be true. As an atheist, I don't rely on religious beliefs to guide me, but I do believe in the power of critical thinking and skepticism. I've also noticed that my high neuroticism score makes me more sensitive to negative news, so I try to limit my exposure to fake or sensational content. I've found that focusing on my hobbies, like travel and football, helps me stay positive and engaged with the real world. I've also learned to use the tools provided by social media platforms to block or report fake content. It's not always easy, but I believe that by being more vigilant and critical, we can all do our part to combat fake news.","reasoning_summary":"The persona's background and traits were used to create a perspective on dealing with fake content on social media. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: In my experience, dealing with fake content on social media can be quite challenging, especially for someone like me who spends several hours daily on these platforms. As a retired man living in Washington DC, I've seen firsthand how misinformation can spread like wildfire, causing unnecessary panic and division. My low openness and conscientiousness scores might make me more susceptible to believing false information, but I've learned to be more critical over time. I remember when I first saw a story about a famous footballer (calcio) being involved in a scandal. It turned out to be completely false, but it had already caused a lot of harm. That's when I realized the importance of verifying information before sharing it. I've since made it a habit to check the source of the information and look for other reliable sources that confirm the story. I also try to avoid sharing anything that seems too sensational or too good to be true. As an atheist, I don't rely on religious beliefs to guide me, but I do believe in the power of critical thinking and skepticism. I've also noticed that my high neuroticism score makes me more sensitive to negative news, so I try to limit my exposure to fake or sensational content. I've found that focusing on my hobbies, like travel and football, helps me stay positive and engaged with the real world. I've also learned to use the tools provided by social media platforms to block or report fake content. It's not always easy, but I believe that by being more vigilant and critical, we can all do our part to combat fake news.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona's background and traits were used to create a perspective on dealing with fake content on social media. The persona's low openness and conscientiousness scores were considered, as they might make him more susceptible to believing false information. The persona's experience with fake news about a famous footballer was used as an example. The persona's atheist beliefs and high neuroticism score were also considered, as they influence his approach to dealing with fake content. The persona's hobbies were mentioned as a way to stay positive and engaged with the real world. The persona's use of social media tools to block or report fake content was also highlighted.
response_parsed.tone: The tone is reflective and practical, with a focus on the persona's personal experiences and strategies for dealing with fake content. The tone is also slightly pessimistic, reflecting the persona's high neuroticism score and depressed psychological feature.
